questions.question: What defines preeclampsia?
options: A. Any blood pressure elevation during pregnancy
B. New-onset hypertension after 20 weeks gestation plus proteinuria, or hypertension plus other organ dysfunction
C. Seizures occurring during pregnancy
D. Gestational diabetes
questions.rationale: Preeclampsia requires new-onset hypertension (typically after 20 weeks) plus proteinuria, or hypertension plus evidence of other organ dysfunction.

questions.question: Is preeclampsia simply 'high blood pressure in pregnancy'?
options: A. Yes, that is the complete definition
B. No — it is a systemic condition that can progress toward severe complications including eclampsia
C. It refers only to blood pressure after delivery
D. It only occurs in the first trimester
questions.rationale: Preeclampsia reflects a broader systemic process, not isolated blood pressure elevation, and can progress to severe complications.

questions.question: What defines eclampsia?
options: A. Preeclampsia plus new seizures or coma not attributable to another cause
B. Any seizure during pregnancy regardless of blood pressure
C. Hypertension alone without other findings
D. Gestational diabetes with seizures
questions.rationale: Eclampsia is defined as preeclampsia with the addition of new seizures or coma that cannot be attributed to another cause.

questions.question: What medication is the definitive hospital/ALS treatment for eclamptic seizures?
options: A. A benzodiazepine alone
B. Magnesium sulfate, per local protocol
C. Insulin
D. Epinephrine
questions.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is the definitive treatment for eclamptic seizures, distinct from typical benzodiazepine-first seizure management.

questions.question: Which finding suggests liver involvement in worsening preeclampsia?
options: A. Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain
B. Ankle swelling alone
C. Mild headache only
D. Normal blood pressure
questions.rationale: Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain suggests liver involvement and is a warning sign of worsening preeclampsia.

questions.question: Which of the following is a warning sign of worsening preeclampsia?
options: A. Severe or persistent headache with visual disturbances
B. Mild fatigue with normal blood pressure
C. Normal proteinuria and normal blood pressure
D. Mild nausea without other findings
questions.rationale: Severe/persistent headache and visual disturbances are recognized warning signs that preeclampsia may be worsening toward severe disease or eclampsia.

questions.question: Why is left lateral positioning used for a pregnant patient late in pregnancy?
options: A. To avoid aortocaval compression from the gravid uterus, which reduces venous return
B. It has no physiologic benefit and is purely for comfort
C. To reduce fetal heart rate
D. To prevent preeclampsia from developing
questions.rationale: Left lateral positioning (or manual uterine displacement) helps avoid aortocaval compression, improving venous return and cardiac output.

questions.question: Why should stimulation (bright lights, loud noise) be minimized in a patient with severe preeclampsia?
options: A. Stimulation can precipitate a seizure in severe preeclampsia
B. It has no clinical relevance
C. It only matters postpartum
D. It is purely a comfort measure with no seizure risk
questions.rationale: Minimizing external stimulation is a recognized precaution in severe preeclampsia because such stimulation can precipitate a seizure.

questions.question: What is the field management for a pregnant patient with suspected preeclampsia?
options: A. Left lateral positioning, minimize stimulation, monitor BP, transport to obstetric-capable facility
B. Supine positioning with no monitoring
C. Withhold transport until symptoms resolve
D. Immediate magnesium sulfate administration by BLS providers
questions.rationale: Field management includes positioning, minimizing stimulation, close BP monitoring, and transport to an obstetric-capable facility.

questions.question: What is the field management for an active eclamptic seizure?
options: A. Standard seizure airway/injury precautions, left lateral positioning once feasible, minimize stimulation, rapid transport
B. Immediate defibrillation
C. Withhold all intervention until hospital arrival
D. Prone positioning throughout
questions.rationale: Eclamptic seizure management follows standard seizure precautions with pregnancy-specific positioning and stimulation considerations, plus rapid transport.

questions.question: How should a new seizure in a pregnant patient past 20 weeks be treated until proven otherwise?
options: A. As a routine seizure unrelated to pregnancy
B. As possible eclampsia
C. As a psychiatric event
D. As a normal finding requiring no action
questions.rationale: A new seizure in this context should be treated as possible eclampsia until proven otherwise, given the severity of the condition.

questions.question: A 32-week pregnant patient reports sudden significant facial and hand swelling along with a blood pressure of 158/102 mmHg. What should this raise concern for?
options: A. Normal pregnancy-related swelling requiring no further evaluation
B. Possible preeclampsia, given the combination of hypertension and sudden significant edema
C. Definite eclampsia, since edema is present
D. A cardiac arrhythmia unrelated to pregnancy
questions.rationale: Sudden significant swelling combined with markedly elevated blood pressure late in pregnancy is a recognized warning sign of preeclampsia.

questions.question: A pregnant patient with known preeclampsia develops a generalized seizure. What should ongoing field priorities include after the seizure resolves?
options: A. No further monitoring is needed once the seizure stops
B. Continued monitoring for repeat seizure and worsening signs, maintaining left lateral positioning during transport
C. Immediate discharge from care
D. Placing the patient supine for the remainder of transport
questions.rationale: After an eclamptic seizure, ongoing monitoring for recurrence and worsening severe-preeclampsia signs is essential, along with maintaining left lateral positioning.

questions.question: Why is it important not to dismiss a pregnant patient's severe headache as a routine complaint?
options: A. Because severe headache is a recognized warning sign of worsening preeclampsia
B. Headaches in pregnancy are never clinically significant
C. Headache is only relevant if accompanied by fever
D. Headache in pregnancy always indicates labor
questions.rationale: A severe or persistent headache is a warning sign that should prompt evaluation for preeclampsia rather than dismissal as routine.

questions.question: What distinguishes eclampsia from preeclampsia?
options: A. Eclampsia requires proteinuria while preeclampsia does not
B. Eclampsia is preeclampsia plus new seizures or coma not attributable to another cause
C. Eclampsia occurs before 20 weeks gestation while preeclampsia occurs after
D. There is no meaningful distinction between the two
questions.rationale: Eclampsia is specifically defined as preeclampsia with the addition of new seizures or coma, representing a severe progression of the same process.
